executive_summary.one_paragraph: CD81 is a tetraspanin (4TM) with two extracellular loops (SEL, LEL) that is robustly surface-accessible across most human cell types. Evidence spans live-cell flow cytometry on primary AML blasts and multiple cell lines (with CRISPR-KO controls), membrane-impermeable surface biotinylation MS on glioblastoma lines and PBMCs, immunogold EM on EVs, and a foundational proteolysis topology assay. Surface expression is broadly constitutive but shows meaningful disease-state modulation: elevated on non-CBF AML blasts (prognostic and LSC-enriched), upregulated under GBM hypoxia, and absent on wild-type HepG2 cells. The tetraspanin web engagement (CD19 co-receptor dependency) and antibody-driven internalization represent the primary accessibility risks.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: CD81 surface accessibility is supported by multiple independent direct methods from multiple independent sources. (1) Live-cell flow cytometry (non-permeabilized) on primary AML blasts (PMC13100155), HEK293T cells and EVs (PMC13098096), and HepG2-hCD81 cells (PMC11423668) — all with paired negative controls. (2) Surface biotinylation using membrane-impermeable Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin on intact glioblastoma cell lines (PMC10898066), EVs (PMC10854524), and PBMCs (PMC8892282), followed by MS or WB. (3) Mass-spec surfaceome confirming CD81 with >2-fold enrichment in at least two biotinylation strategies (PMC12749419). (4) Immunogold EM directly visualizing CD81 on EV outer membranes (PMC12927977). (5) Foundational functional topology assay (proteolysis of microsome-embedded protein) establishing extracellular loop accessibility (PMID:1860863). (6) Antibody-mediated surface down-modulation on live hematolymphoid cells (PMID:2398277). No meaningful contradictions identified; cytosol annotation in HPA is consistent with known tetraspanin intracellular pool and does not conflict with surface presence.

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (surface staining, PMC11423668)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by paired WT HepG2 (absent signal) vs hCD81-overexpressing HepG2 (high signal) in same flow panel.

expression_observations.context: Wild-type HepG2 cells — no detectable CD81 surface expression (negative control)
HepG2 cells engineered to overexpress hCD81 — high CD81 surface expression

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (surface staining, PMC13100155)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by positive correlation (r²=0.51) between CD81 mRNA and flow cytometry surface signal across AML patient cohort.

expression_observations.context: AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis — representative histograms with blasts (green) and hematogones (purple); bimodal distribution of CD81 surface expression in cohort n=290

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (surface staining, PMC13098096)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by paired WT vs single-cell CRISPR KO of tetraspanins; KO cells lose surface signal.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T wild-type cells — positive for CD81 on cell surface by flow cytometry
HEK293T wild-type-derived extracellular vesicles (WT-EVs) — positive for CD81 on EV surface

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (surface staining, PMC11256977)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used in functional trafficking assay; CD81 co-transfection enhances CD19-APEX2 surface delivery ~3-fold, confirming CD81 plasma membrane localization.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T cells co-transfected with CD19-APEX2 and CD81 — 3-fold increase in CD19 cell surface staining vs CD19-APEX2 alone, confirming CD81 traffics to plasma membrane

antibodies.name: MACSPlex Exosome Kit anti-CD81 bead capture
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Part of 37-surface-epitope multiplex bead panel with isotype controls; vendor-validated kit for EV surface marker detection.

expression_observations.context: Small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) characterized by MACSPlex Exosome Kit — CD81 among 37 surface epitopes detected
EVs derived from unspecified cell lines — immunogold EM visualizes and quantifies CD81 on outer EV membrane surface
Glioblastoma cell lines (U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG) — EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin surface labeling of intact cells for surfaceome proteomics
Extracellular vesicles (mEVs and sEVs) — EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (10 mM) labels surface-accessible proteins on intact EV pellets; CD81 identified in surface proteomics

Unspecified cell line (GCPII-expressing) — CD81 identified by MS with >2-fold enrichment in surface biotinylation, confirmed by ≥2 independent biotinylation strategies; co-enriched with CD9, ITGB1, THY1, PTGFRN

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (WB validation of EV surface fractionation, PMC10614838)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: CD81 used as canonical EV marker loading control in WB to validate EV surface protein isolation workflow.

expression_observations.context: WI-38 fibroblasts and derived EVs — CD81 detected in EV surface protein fractions by WB as canonical EV surface marker; UniProt annotation confirms membrane/cell surface classification

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (WB, Santa Cruz SC-166029)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used at 1:500 dilution for WB detection of CD81 in EV fractions; absence of calnexin (ER marker) confirms fractionation purity.

expression_observations.context: Unspecified EV fractions — CD81 enriched in EV fractions alongside CD63 and CD9; calnexin absent, confirming minimal organelle contamination

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (rabbit, Abcam ab219209)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Rabbit polyclonal used at 1:200 on glioblastoma tissue sections and PBMCs; no independent validation strategy reported.

expression_observations.context: Glioblastoma tissue sections and PBMCs from glioblastoma study — CD81 detected by IHC/IF

antibodies.name: anti-TAPA-1/CD81 (mAb 5A6, IgG1)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Raised against human B lymphoma cells; IgG1 mAb used for IP yielding 26 kDa band; CHAPS vs Triton X-100 detergent comparison reveals co-precipitated complex partners.

expression_observations.context: Hematolymphoid cell lines — CD81 immunoprecipitated as 26 kDa single chain in Triton X-100 lysate; CHAPS lysate co-precipitates Leu-13 and additional proteins suggesting plasma membrane complex

antibodies.name: anti-TAPA-1/CD81 (mAb 5A6, IgG1)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Raised against human B lymphoma cells by immunization; selected for growth inhibition. Targets LEL (second extracellular domain) of CD81.

expression_observations.context: Hematolymphoid cell lines — antibody-mediated down-modulation of TAPA-1/CD81 from plasma membrane; reciprocal co-modulation with Leu-13 confirms surface complex
Most human cell lines — CD81 (TAPA-1) described as 26 kDa cell surface protein expressed broadly
HPA database curated annotation — CD81 subcellular location: Plasma membrane and Cytosol

non_surface_expression.context: AML bone marrow blasts — CD81 mRNA levels correlated with surface flow cytometry signal (r²=0.51) across a cohort; mRNA used as a surrogate in independent validation cohorts
Hematolymphoid cell lines — immunoprecipitation from whole-cell lysate (non-surface-specific); CD81 detected as 26 kDa band, also present in intracellular/complex fractions depending on detergent
WI-38 lung fibroblast cell line — UniProt-annotated membrane/surface proteins from MS dataset; CD81 categorized as transmembrane protein among 136 membrane proteins
Glioblastoma tissue / PBMCs — IHC/IF with anti-CD81 antibody (Abcam ab219209, 1:200) on fixed tissue sections; permeabilization status unspecified

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Most human cell types with constitutive CD81 expression
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Same cells; HPA annotation identifies both plasma membrane and cytosolic pools
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 is distributed between the plasma membrane and a cytosolic intracellular pool, meaning only a fraction of total cellular CD81 is surface-exposed at any given time.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Binders targeting the extracellular loops will only access the plasma membrane fraction; intracellular CD81 is inaccessible to non-permeabilized reagents and intact-cell therapeutics.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal hematopoietic progenitors / hematogones in bone marrow with moderate or low CD81 surface expression
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: AML blasts (especially non-CBF AML and NPM1-mutant AML) at diagnosis
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 surface levels are significantly elevated on non-CBF AML blasts compared to CBF-AML blasts and normal hematogones; high surface CD81 correlates with adverse prognosis and enrichment in leukemia stem cell-containing subpopulations.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: High surface CD81 on non-CBF AML blasts makes CD81 a tractable antibody or ADC target in this disease context; CBF-AML blasts with low CD81 would be poorly targeted by anti-CD81 agents.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Wild-type HepG2 hepatocellular carcinoma cells with absent CD81 surface expression
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: HepG2 cells engineered to overexpress human CD81
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 surface expression transitions from undetectable on wild-type HepG2 to high-level surface expression upon forced overexpression, demonstrating that the hepatocyte-lineage cell does not constitutively traffic CD81 to the surface.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: In hepatocyte-derived tumor cells lacking endogenous surface CD81, anti-CD81 therapeutic agents would have negligible target accessibility unless CD81 is artificially supplied or upregulated by disease-specific mechanisms.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: GBM cell lines under normoxic conditions
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: GBM cell lines under hypoxic stress
accessibility_modulation.change: Hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome; CD81 is identified as a surface-accessible ADC target candidate specifically in the hypoxic condition, implying upregulation or redistribution to the surface under hypoxia.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD81 surface accessibility on GBM cells is enhanced under hypoxia (a common tumor microenvironment condition), making it a potential ADC target in the hypoxic tumor core where other targets may be downregulated.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Low-grade brain tumors assessed by tumor surfaceome profiling
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: High-grade brain tumors (gliomas) assessed by the same surfaceome profiling (TS-MAP/SURFME)
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 surface abundance diverges between high-grade and low-grade brain tumors, with differential normalized relative abundance identified by mass-spectrometry-based surfaceome profiling.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD81 surface accessibility is modulated by tumor grade in gliomas; targeting CD81 may be more or less effective depending on tumor grade, necessitating grade-stratified patient selection for CD81-directed therapies.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Resting hematolymphoid cells with constitutive CD81 surface expression
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Hematolymphoid cells treated with anti-CD81 or anti-Leu-13 antibody
accessibility_modulation.change: Surface CD81 is down-modulated upon antibody binding, and CD81 and Leu-13 reciprocally co-modulate each other's surface expression, indicating receptor-occupancy-driven internalization or shedding.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Anti-CD81 antibody binding triggers loss of surface CD81, which could limit sustained target engagement by therapeutic antibodies and necessitates consideration of receptor turnover in dosing strategies.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Most somatic tissues where CD81 is broadly expressed at the plasma membrane
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Testicular germ cells within seminiferous tubules showing both cytoplasmic and membranous CD81 expression
accessibility_modulation.change: IHC reveals CD81 has both cytoplasmic and membranous localization specifically in testicular germ cells, used as a positive control for EV marker staining, indicating a distinct dual distribution in this germline tissue.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: In testicular germ cells, CD81 is partially membrane-associated and partially cytoplasmic; surface accessibility exists but may be lower than in cells with predominantly membrane-localized CD81.

structure.source: AlphaFold DB (STUB — fetchers not yet built)

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: CD81 is required for surface trafficking of CD19 in B cells; CD81-deficient cells fail to deliver CD19 to the plasma membrane. Conversely, CD81 itself is broadly surface-expressed independent of CD19. Leu-13 co-modulates CD81 surface levels upon antibody treatment, suggesting a co-regulatory complex, but CD81 surface expression does not strictly require Leu-13.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: Tetraspanins are known to organize into tetraspanin-enriched microdomains (TEMs) and associate with lipid rafts, which could restrict epitope access in dense complexes. No direct evidence in the ledger demonstrates biologically consequential raft-restriction for anti-CD81 binder access; surface biotinylation and flow cytometry detect CD81 without raft-disruption pretreatment.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: CD81 has a small extracellular loop (SEL, ~30 residues) and a large extracellular loop (LEL, ~89 residues, aa 113-201). The LEL is the primary accessible domain and has been crystallized (PDB 1G8Q, 5DFV); its folded mushroom-like structure provides a defined but compact epitope surface for binders.

epitope_masking.rationale: CD81 LEL epitopes can be occluded by tetraspanin web partners (CD9, CD19, Leu-13) in the tetraspanin-enriched microdomain. Antibody-induced co-modulation of Leu-13 and CD81 demonstrates partner-dependent epitope dynamics. The LEL adopts a compact folded structure that may restrict access to linear epitopes; HCV E2 and anti-CD81 mAb 5A6 target overlapping LEL subregions, confirming conformational dependency.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) is identified as a 26-kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines, establishing its baseline plasma membrane accessibility in a foundational characterization paper from 1991.

spans.quote: TAPA-1 (the target of an antiproliferative antibody) is a 26-kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) belongs to a tetraspanin family of cell surface proteins defined by four transmembrane domains, placing it in the 4TM topology class with extracellular-accessible loops.

spans.quote: TAPA-1 is a member of an evolutionarily related family of cell surface proteins all of which contain four transmembrane domains.

evidence.claim: Topology of CD81 (TAPA-1) was validated by proteolysis studies on in vitro translated protein embedded in microsomal membranes — a functional topology assay confirming membrane insertion and extracellular loop accessibility.

cell_context.material_kind_other_label: in vitro translated protein in microsomal membranes

spans.quote: A model is proposed for topology of TAPA-1 based on proteolysis studies in the in vitro translated protein embedded into microsomal membranes.

evidence.claim: CD81 topology model: N- and C-termini are cytoplasmic; two hydrophilic loop regions are extracellular. This defines a small extracellular loop (SEL, aa 34–63) and a large extracellular loop (LEL, aa 113–201) as accessible from the extracellular face.

spans.quote: This analysis predicts that the amino and the carboxyl termini of the molecule are cytoplasmic and that the two hydrophilic regions of the molecule are extracellular.

evidence.claim: The antigenic epitope of human CD81 is contained within the second extracellular domain (LEL), confirming this region is surface-accessible and amenable to antibody binding at the plasma membrane.

spans.quote: The antigenic epitope of the human TAPA-1 is contained within a subregion of the second extracellular domain of the protein.

evidence.claim: Murine monoclonal antibody 5A6 (IgG1 class) was raised against human B lymphoma cells and selected for growth inhibition; it targets a cell-surface accessible epitope on CD81. This antibody is a primary reagent for surface detection of CD81 in multiple assay formats.

spans.quote: A murine mAb, 5A6 (IgG1), has been isolated by immunization with a human B lymphoma cell line and screening for growth inhibition.

evidence.claim: Cell-surface expression of TAPA-1 (CD81) was directly demonstrated by antibody-mediated down-modulation from the plasma membrane in hematolymphoid cell lines; co-modulation with Leu-13 confirms CD81 and Leu-13 co-occupy the cell surface as a complex.

spans.quote: The cell-surface expression of both TAPA-1 (target of an antiproliferative antibody-1) and Leu-13 could be down-modulated by binding to their respective antibodies and they could be reciprocally comodulated.

evidence.claim: Crystal structure of the human CD81 large extracellular loop (LEL/CD81-LEL) has been solved (PDB entries 1G8Q, 1IV5, 5DFV etc.) and was used for structural docking studies, confirming the LEL projects into the extracellular space and is structurally defined at atomic resolution.

spans.quote: Because Ser 528 in epitope III is interchangeable with asparagine or threonine in the natural HCV variants, we tested Asn 528 - and Thr 528 -substituted peptides, assuming they take the same conformation displayed on the E2 structure, to see if they could dock onto the crystal structure of the human CD81 large extracellular domain (CD81-LEL) by using the Rosetta FastRelax protocols ( Fig. 5 ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry on live cells was used to validate surface expression of CD81 in HepG2 cells engineered to overexpress hCD81; this is the primary surface-detection readout in this study, using intact non-permeabilized cells.

spans.quote: The resulting cells were validated by flow cytometry for surface expression of CD81 ( Figure 1a ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry histograms directly demonstrate that wild-type HepG2 cells lack detectable CD81 surface expression (absent signal), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high surface CD81. This paired positive/negative result validates the surface flow assay specificity for CD81.

spans.quote: As previously reported [ 12 ], CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells (grey histogram), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high CD81 surface expression (red histogram).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured on AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis — direct surface-accessibility measurement by flow cytometry on primary human AML cells using intact non-permeabilized cells.

spans.quote: Fig. 1 High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: CD81 mRNA levels correlate positively (r²=0.51) with CD81 cell surface abundance determined by flow cytometry across a cohort of AML patients, independently validating the flow cytometry surface-detection assay and confirming CD81 surface protein reflects transcriptional output.

spans.quote: In order to independently validate this finding, we applied the positive correlation found in our cohort between CD81 mRNA and cell surface abundance determined by flow cytometry (FCM) (Fig. 1c ) on two independent cohorts of AML.

evidence.claim: Bimodal distribution of CD81 surface expression detected on AML blasts by flow cytometry in a large cohort (n=290), demonstrating heterogeneous but measurable surface accessibility of CD81 on primary human leukemia cells.

spans.quote: Similarly, we detected a bimodal distribution of CD81 surface expression on blasts in this larger cohort (Supplementary Methods and Fig. 2 ; Fig. 1a ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry on live HEK293T cells and derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) shows CD81 is present on the surface of both wild-type cells and wild-type EVs, confirming plasma membrane localization by non-permeabilized flow cytometry.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis showed that HEK293T WT cells and WT‐EVs were positive for CD9, CD63, and CD81 on their surface (Figure 2B,C ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry was used as the validation method for tetraspanin (CD9, CD63, CD81) surface expression on cells and EVs in a single-cell CRISPR knockout study, providing paired WT vs KO surface signal as a validation strategy.

spans.quote: Flow cytometry validation of tetraspanins surface expression on the cell and the EVs after single‐cell knockout of tetraspanins.

evidence.claim: Functional trafficking assay in HEK293T cells demonstrates that CD81 co-transfection enhances delivery of CD19 to the cell surface by ~3-fold (measured by surface staining via flow cytometry), confirming CD81 itself traffics to and resides at the plasma membrane where it functions as a chaperone for CD19 surface transport.

spans.quote: Properly folded CD19 is trafficked to the cell surface by forming a complex with the tetraspanin CD81. 29 As an initial test for the functional integrity of our fusion construct, we used a trafficking assay in HEK293T cells to assess the ability of CD81 to enhance the delivery of CD19-APEX2 to the cell surface. 30 , 31 Our CD19-APEX2 protein matches the response of wild-type CD19, showing a 3-fold increase in cell surface staining when co-transfected with CD81 ( Figures S1B and S1C ), confirming that the fusion protein was properly transported to the cell surface and that its interaction with

evidence.claim: MACSPlex Exosome Kit flow cytometry panel simultaneously detects 37 surface epitopes on small EVs, including CD81, using bead-based capture and isotype controls; this documents a validated, multiplex flow cytometry method for CD81 surface detection on EVs with isotype controls included.

spans.quote: Isolated sEVs were characterized by flow cytometry using the MACSPlex Exosome Kit (Miltenyi Biotec, Bergisch Gladbach, Germany), which was developed for the simultaneous detection of 37 surface epitopes (CD1c, CD2, CD3, CD4, CD8, CD9, CD11c, CD14, CD19, CD20, CD24, CD25, CD29, CD31, CD40, CD41b, CD42a, CD44, CD45, CD49e, CD56, CD62P, CD63, CD69, CD81, CD86, CD105, CD133, CD142, CD146, CD209, CD326, HLA-ABC, HLA-DRDPDQ, MCSP, ROR1 and SSEA-4) that are known to be present on different exosomes and two isotype control beads.

evidence.claim: Immunogold electron microscopy (iEM) was used to directly visualize and quantify surface expression of CD81 and CD9 on EVs derived from cell lines, providing ultrastructural-resolution evidence of CD81 on the outer membrane surface of extracellular vesicles.

spans.quote: To further characterize the EVs derived from cell lines, we performed immunogold EM (iEM) to visualize and quantify the surface expression of CD9 and CD81.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation protocol using membrane-impermeable EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin (1 mg/mL, 30 min, pH 8) on intact cells followed by glycine quench; this is a canonical non-permeabilizing surface-capture method confirming only plasma-membrane-exposed proteins are labeled.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: glioblastoma cell lines (U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG)

spans.quote: Cells were washed with PBS (with MgCl 2 and CaCl 2 Mg/Ca-PBS) at pH 8 prior to incubation with a 1 mg/mL solution of membrane-impermeable biotin (EZ-Link Sulfo-N-hydroxysuccinimide-SS-biotin, Thermo Scientific, 21331) for 30 min, rinsed, incubated with 0.1 M glycine for 10 min to stop the reaction, and rinsed again prior to processing of surface biotinylated proteins (surfaceome).

evidence.claim: Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (EZ-Link, 10 mM) surface labeling applied to EV pellets after ultracentrifugation; membrane-impermeable reagent labels only surface-exposed proteins on intact EVs, providing a surface-capture step for EV surfaceome proteomics.

spans.quote: Following the first ultracentrifugation steps for mEVs and sEVs at 16,500 g and 100,000 g, respectively, pellets were resuspended in 10 mM EZ-Link™ Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (Cat No. 21331, ThermoFisher Scientific, Mississauga, ON, Canada) in PBS, pH 7.4.

evidence.claim: Surface-accessible proteins on EVs were enriched by Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin labeling followed by proteomics analysis; CD81 is one of the proteins identified through this surface-capture enrichment approach, confirming its presence on the EV surface accessible to extracellular reagents.

spans.quote: Proteomics samples were then analyzed using two approaches based on (1) global proteomic analysis and (2) enrichment of EV surface proteins by labeling surface-accessible proteins with a Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin reagent.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation mass spectrometry (MS) identified CD81 as one of nine proteins enriched in the GCPII surface-capture proteomics experiment, alongside canonical surface markers CD9, ITGB1, THY1, and PTGFRN, confirming CD81 plasma membrane surface accessibility by MS-based surfaceome analysis.

spans.quote: MS analysis identified GCPII as the most enriched protein, along with eight additional proteins previously detected in proximity labeling experiments: CD81 (CD81 antigen), CD9 (CD9 antigen), GPM6A (neuronal membrane glycoprotein M6-a), ITGB1 (integrin β1), MYADM (myeloid-associated differentiation marker), PROCR (endothelial protein C receptor), PTGFRN (prostaglandin F2 receptor negative regulator), and THY1 (Thy-1 membrane glycoprotein) (Fig. 3 B).

evidence.claim: CD81 shows >2-fold enrichment in surface biotinylation and was confirmed by at least two independent biotinylation strategies, providing high-confidence methodological validation of its plasma membrane surface accessibility by orthogonal surface-capture MS approaches.

spans.quote: Eight proteins exhibiting > 2-fold enrichment and identified previously by at least two biotinylation strategies are marked in blue (CD81, CD9, GPM6A, ITGB1, MYADM, PROCR, PTGFRN, and THY1).

evidence.claim: Streptavidin bead enrichment of surface-biotinylated proteins followed by Western blot analysis — this paired surface-biotinylation + WB step is the methodological backbone confirming the surface-capture workflow used to identify CD81 as a surface protein.

spans.quote: Following cell lysis and protein enrichment on streptavidin beads, we performed Western blot analysis of the resulting enriched proteins.

evidence.claim: Surface proteins were isolated from cells and extracellular vesicles for mass spectrometry and western blot analysis — this paired surface-protein isolation + MS/WB workflow is the methodological step that produced the CD81 surface proteomics data in this paper.

spans.quote: Surface proteins on cells and EVs were isolated for mass spectrometry (MS) and western blot analysis.

evidence.claim: UniProt-based annotation of MS proteomics results categorized CD81 among 136 proteins (of 456 total) classified as membrane, cell membrane, or cell surface proteins — with CD81 carrying transmembrane domains — confirming its assignment as a surface/membrane protein in this proteomics dataset.

spans.quote: Uniprot categorized 136 of the 456 proteins (29.82%) proteins as being present in membranes, on the cell membrane, or on the cell surface; 65 of them, including CD81, CD9, and CD63, have at least one (and up to 12) transmembrane domains ( Dataset S15 ).

evidence.claim: Western blot of CD81 as an EV marker was performed in HeLa whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein preparations — CD81 serves as a loading/marker control confirming successful EV surface protein fractionation. This is a paired WB step anchoring the surface-fractionation workflow.

spans.quote: We first validated the production of DPP4-Myc fusion protein, along with loading control GAPDH and EV marker CD81, in HeLa whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein preparations.

evidence.claim: Anti-CD81 antibody (Santa Cruz #SC-166029, 1:500) used for Western blot detection of CD81 in EV fractionation experiments; this documents the antibody clone/catalog number for methodological annotation of CD81 WB assays in this EV surfaceome study.

spans.quote: Membranes were then incubated with the following primary antibodies overnight at 4°C: CD63 (1:1000, EMD Millipore #CBL553), CD81 (1:500, Santa Cruz #SC‐166029), CD9 (1:500, Cell Signaling #D801A), Calnexin (1:1000, Cell signaling #2433S), Annexin 1 (1:500, Santa Cruz #SC‐12740), HSP90B1 (1:1000, Cell Signaling #7411), and Streptavidin HRP (1:10,000, Cell Signaling #3999) all diluted in 5% BSA.

evidence.claim: Western blot shows CD81 (along with CD63 and CD9) enriched in EV fractions with absence of ER marker calnexin, validating EV surface fractionation purity. CD81 here functions as a canonical EV surface marker confirming its membrane surface localization on EVs.

spans.quote: Western blot analysis revealed EV markers (CD81, CD63 and CD9) in EV fractions and the absence of the endoplasmic reticulum marker calnexin, suggesting minimal contamination from cellular organelles (Figure 1G ).

evidence.claim: Anti-CD81 antibody (Abcam ab219209, rabbit, 1:200) used for IF/IHC on tissue sections — this documents the antibody clone/catalog number and dilution for IHC/IF detection of CD81 in a glioblastoma EV study.

spans.quote: Thermo Fisher Scientific, PA5-75459), mouse anti-CD63 (1/100, Abcam, ab8219), mouse anti-CD45-PE (1/25, BD Biosciences, 555483), rabbit anti-CD68 (1/800, Cell Signaling Technology, D4B9C), mouse anti-CD31 (1/100, Dako, JC70A), mouse anti-CD44 (1/100, Dako, DF1485), mouse anti-CD14-PE (1/25, BioLegend), rabbit anti-BCAN (1/200, Abcam, ab111719), rabbit anti-CD81 (1/200, Abcam, ab219209), rabbit anti-TF (kindly provided by Wolfram Ruf, Center for Thrombosis and Hemostasis, University of Mainz, Mainz, Germany), or rabbit anti-MCT2 (1/100, Thermo Fisher Scientific, PA5-112712).

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation of PBMCs is explicitly named as a surface-capture method in this study's methods summary, confirming CD81 surface isolation from primary human immune cells (PBMCs) using biotinylation-based fractionation.

spans.quote: Detailed descriptions of the mouse GBM model, Western blotting, ligand uptake, biotinylation of PBMCs and CD14 + magnetic cell separation sorting, Incucyte cytotox assay, and GSEA and pathway analysis of LC-MS/MS data are listed in SI Appendix .

evidence.claim: HPA database annotates CD81 subcellular location as Plasma membrane and Cytosol, providing a curated database-level surface accessibility assignment.

spans.quote: HPA subcellular locations: Plasma membrane;Cytosol.

evidence.claim: HPA explicitly flags CD81 as plasma-membrane accessible in its curated database annotation, supporting surface classification.

spans.quote: HPA flags this gene as plasma-membrane accessible.

evidence.claim: Immunoprecipitation of CD81 (26 kDa) from cell lysates using antibody 5A6; detergent comparison (Triton X-100 vs CHAPS) reveals that CHAPS co-precipitates additional proteins (including Leu-13), suggesting CD81 exists in a multi-protein complex at the plasma membrane. This is a non-permeabilizing whole-cell lysis assay.

spans.quote: The antibody immunoprecipitated a single chain protein of 26 kDa from cell lysates made with Triton X-100 but additional proteins were precipitated when cell lysates were made with the milder detergent CHAPS (3-[3-cholamidopropyl)dimethylammonio)-1-propane sulfate).

evidence.claim: HPA database annotates CD81 subcellular localization as plasma membrane and cytosol, indicating dual compartment assignment — consistent with cell surface presence but also intracellular pool.

spans.quote: HPA subcellular locations: Plasma membrane;Cytosol.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) is a 26-kDa cell surface protein expressed broadly on most human cell lines, indicating near-ubiquitous surface expression across human cell types.

spans.quote: TAPA-1 (the target of an antiproliferative antibody) is a 26-kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) is expressed on the surface of hematolymphoid cell lines, co-localizing with the Leu-13 antigen, establishing CD81 surface expression in the hematopoietic lineage.

spans.quote: We have identified one of these coprecipitated molecules as the 16-kDa Leu-13 Ag. 5A6 and anti-Leu-13 showed similar, although not identical, reactivity, growth inhibition and temperature-dependent aggregation effects among hematolymphoid cell lines.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) and Leu-13 form a complex on the cell surface of hematolymphoid cells and are functionally linked to growth control, confirming surface complex formation in hematopoietic cells.

spans.quote: These results suggest that TAPA-1 and Leu-13 form a complex on the cell surface and play a role in growth control through a common pathway.

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression on hematolymphoid cells can be down-modulated by antibody binding and is reciprocally co-modulated with Leu-13, demonstrating antibody-induced accessibility modulation at the cell surface. Baseline: resting hematolymphoid cells with constitutive surface CD81. Modulating state: antibody engagement. Change: surface CD81 downregulated. Implication: surface accessibility is dynamically regulated by receptor occupancy.

spans.quote: The cell-surface expression of both TAPA-1 (target of an antiproliferative antibody-1) and Leu-13 could be down-modulated by binding to their respective antibodies and they could be reciprocally comodulated.

evidence.claim: A patient with a homozygous CD81 loss-of-function mutation had a complete absence of CD81 protein expression on blood leukocytes, establishing that CD81 is normally expressed on the surface of blood leukocytes (B cells and related populations) in healthy humans.

spans.quote: The patient had normal CD19 alleles but carried a homozygous CD81 mutation resulting in a complete lack of CD81 expression on blood leukocytes.

evidence.claim: CD81 expression on B cells is required for CD19 membrane expression; in CD81-deficient EBV-transformed B cells, CD19 fails to reach the plasma membrane, demonstrating that CD81 controls surface accessibility of the CD19–CD21 co-receptor complex on B cells.

spans.quote: Retroviral transduction and glycosylation experiments on EBV-transformed B cells from the patient revealed that CD19 membrane expression critically depended on CD81.

evidence.claim: CD81-deficient patient B cells show impaired activation upon BCR stimulation (phenocopying CD19 deficiency), while T cell subsets and function are normal, indicating that CD81 surface expression is functionally critical on B cells but not required for T cell surface identity.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: B cells and T cells from CD81-deficient patient

spans.quote: Similar to CD19-deficient patients, CD81-deficient patients had B cells that showed impaired activation upon stimulation via the B cell antigen receptor but no overt T cell subset or function defects.

evidence.claim: CD81 traffics CD19 to the cell surface: co-transfection of CD81 with CD19-APEX2 in HEK293T cells yields a 3-fold increase in CD19 surface staining, confirming that CD81 is required for surface delivery of the CD19 receptor complex. CD81 itself is expressed at the surface of these cells as the trafficking scaffold.

spans.quote: Properly folded CD19 is trafficked to the cell surface by forming a complex with the tetraspanin CD81. 29 As an initial test for the functional integrity of our fusion construct, we used a trafficking assay in HEK293T cells to assess the ability of CD81 to enhance the delivery of CD19-APEX2 to the cell surface. 30 , 31 Our CD19-APEX2 protein matches the response of wild-type CD19, showing a 3-fold increase in cell surface staining when co-transfected with CD81 ( Figures S1B and S1C ), confirming that the fusion protein was properly transported to the cell surface and that its interaction with

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 hepatocellular carcinoma cells but is high on HepG2 cells engineered to overexpress human CD81, indicating that the hepatocyte-derived cell line does not constitutively express CD81 at the surface without forced overexpression.

spans.quote: As previously reported [ 12 ], CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells (grey histogram), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high CD81 surface expression (red histogram).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry of AML bone marrow at diagnosis shows CD81 surface expression on AML blasts (green) relative to corresponding hematogones (purple), establishing that CD81 is detectable at the surface of AML bone marrow blasts.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML bone marrow blasts and hematogones

spans.quote: High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression measured by flow cytometry is significantly lower in core-binding factor (CBF) AML (n=26, favorable prognosis) compared to non-CBF AML (n=226), demonstrating disease-subtype-dependent modulation of CD81 surface levels across AML bone marrow samples (n=290 total).

cell_context.disease_state: acute myeloid leukemia — CBF vs. non-CBF subtypes

spans.quote: The violin plot in the right panel summarizes low vs . high CD81 in the total AML study group (n = 290). b Violin plot comparing CD81 cell surface expression in CBF vs . non-CBF AML (n = 26, n = 226) and c scatterplot correlating CD81 z-score normalized mRNA vs .

evidence.claim: CBF-AML (favorable prognosis, n=26) have lower CD81 cell surface expression compared to non-CBF AML, establishing that CD81 surface levels stratify by AML genetic subtype and correlate inversely with prognosis.

spans.quote: In this study, we show that core-binding factor (CBF) AML (n = 26), which have well-known favorable prognosis, 3 have lower CD81 cell surface expression compared to non-CBF AML (Fig. 1b , Supplementary Fig. 3a ).

evidence.claim: CBF-AML have significantly lower CD81 mRNA expression compared to non-CBF AML across two independent cohorts (Leucegene and Beat AML datasets), corroborating the surface protein data and demonstrating transcriptional regulation of CD81 levels by AML subtype.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML bone marrow blasts — Leucegene and Beat AML cohorts

cell_context.disease_state: acute myeloid leukemia — CBF vs. non-CBF

spans.quote: Consistent with our results, CBF-AML have significant lower CD81 mRNA expression compared to non-CBF AML shown for the Leucegene cohort 33 and the Beat AML dataset 34 (Fig. 1d ).

evidence.claim: High CD81 mRNA expression at AML diagnosis adversely affects overall survival in non-CBF AML (HR 1.74) and NPM1-mutant AML (HR 4.18) in the Beat AML dataset, while CD81 expression does not differ among other major AML subtypes, indicating disease-state-specific prognostic relevance of CD81 expression.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML bone marrow blasts — Beat AML dataset

cell_context.disease_state: acute myeloid leukemia — non-CBF and NPM1-mutant subtypes

spans.quote: Despite the exclusion of good prognosis CBF-AML and that CD81 expression was not different among other major AML subtypes (Supplementary Fig. 4 ), high diagnostic CD81 mRNA expression adversely affected overall survival (OS) in non-CBF AML and in NPM1- mutant AML using the Beat AML dataset (Fig. 1e ; non-CBF AML: HR [CI] = 1.74 [0.97–3.12]; NPM1 -mutant AML: HR [CI] = 4.18 [1.42–12.36]). 35 , 36 Of note, AML with NPM1 mutation showed lower CD81 expression, although this did not reach statistical significance

evidence.claim: CD81 is enriched within leukemia stem cell (LSC)-containing subpopulations in AML bone marrow, where its surface expression supports LSC maintenance and resistance to chemotherapy, placing CD81 surface accessibility in a therapy-resistant, quiescent stem cell state.

spans.quote: Notably, CD81 was enriched within LSC-containing subpopulations, where its expression supported LSC maintenance and resistance to chemotherapy.

evidence.claim: CD81 shows cytoplasmic and membranous expression in germ cells within seminiferous tubules of the testis, serving as a positive control for EV marker IHC in retinoblastoma tissue study. This establishes CD81 protein expression (membrane-associated) in testicular germ cells.

spans.quote: CD81: Cytoplasmic and membranous expression of the germ cells within the seminiferous tubules was considered as positive control ( Fig. 1 c1).

evidence.claim: CD81 IHC was performed on ovarian cancer (OvCa) tissue sections as an exosomal surface marker to assess exosome-mediated premetastatic niche formation, establishing CD81 protein expression in ovarian cancer tumor tissue.

spans.quote: We have performed IHC analysis for exosomal surface marker CD81 in OvCa tissues as exosomes participate in preparing a premetastatic niche (PMN) for the migrating cancer cells at the secondary site and promote metastasis [ 32 ].

evidence.claim: CD81 surface abundance diverges between high-grade and low-grade brain tumors as measured by tumor surfaceome profiling (TS-MAP/SURFME), suggesting that CD81 surface accessibility is modulated by tumor grade in gliomas.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: high-grade vs. low-grade brain tumor tissue

spans.quote: We selected EGFR, BCAN, CD81, TF, and MCT2 for further studies based on their divergent, normalized relative abundances between high- and low-grade tumors according to TS-MAP/SURFME data ( Fig. 3 F ).

evidence.claim: Hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM (glioblastoma multiforme) surface-endocytome, and CD81 is validated as a potential ADC target candidate in GBM cell lines under hypoxic conditions, indicating disease-state/stress-induced modulation of CD81 surface accessibility in GBM.

spans.quote: We reveal how hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome, and validate several potential ADC target candidates; CXADR, CD47, CD81, BSG, and FXYD6.

evidence.claim: CD81 is detected as a positive extracellular vesicle (EV) marker by western blot in whole cell lysates and EV fractions from brain tumor-initiating cell (BTIC) lines (612, 120, 1a), establishing CD81 protein expression in glioblastoma stem-like cells.

spans.quote: (G) Western blot for brain tumour‐initiating cell (BTIC) lines (612, 120, 1a) WCL, EV, and VFM probing for positive (CD81, CD63, CD9), negative (calnexin) EV markers.

confidence_reasoning: A1 grades the evidence as direct_multi_method with zero contradicting entries. Multiple orthogonal direct methods (live-cell flow with CRISPR-KO controls, membrane-impermeable surface biotinylation MS confirmed by ≥2 independent strategies, immunogold EM, and a foundational proteolysis topology assay) converge unambiguously on high surface accessibility. A2 introduces moderate state-dependence (absent on wild-type HepG2, elevated in non-CBF AML and hypoxic GBM) but does not contradict the core surface accessibility claim — these are context-specific quantitative differences, not evidence against surface presence. The HPA cytosol co-annotation reflects the known tetraspanin intracellular trafficking pool and is consistent with simultaneous plasma-membrane localization. No dominant secreted or shed form is identified. Integrated confidence is high.
